---
title: "A Review of Naturally Occurring Food Allergens and Their Impact on Health"
author: "Renu Indhikkattu Chittoor, Harikumaran Thampi Balakrishnan Saraswathi"
year: 2022
doi: "10.13005/bbra/2965"
journal: "Biosciences Biotechnology Research Asia"
volume: "19(1):13–35"
pmid: "DON'T KNOW"
access: "Open access — CC BY 4.0. PDF accessed directly."
source_quality: "Full text read from PDF."
---

# A Review of Naturally Occurring Food Allergens and Their Impact on Health

**Authors:** Renu Indhikkattu Chittoor, Harikumaran Thampi Balakrishnan Saraswathi* (Department of Life Sciences, University of Calicut, Kerala, India)
**Year:** 2022 (received 7 Dec 2021; accepted 27 Jan 2022)
**DOI:** 10.13005/bbra/2965
**Journal:** Biosciences Biotechnology Research Asia, 19(1):13–35

## Why this paper

Brought in for India-specific comparison of hypersensitivity vs sensitisation rates in children. Note on journal: Biosciences Biotechnology Research Asia is a low-impact Indian open-access journal, not indexed in PubMed. The paper is a narrative review drawing on other sources rather than presenting original data. Value: it frames the sensitisation-reactivity paradox explicitly and reviews allergen characterisation methods.

## Assessment of source quality

Reading the full PDF confirms the earlier assessment: this paper does not present independent Indian data. The four key India figures it cites (0.14% food allergy prevalence, 19.1% sensitisation, 0.03% peanut allergy, 6.3% peanut sensitisation) trace to the same underlying EuroPrevall-INCO studies cited in P04 (Krishna 2020) and P02 (Mahesh 2023b). The paper's phrasing is sometimes awkward ("harmful safe reactions to food items", "turn of the events") suggesting translation or non-native English composition.

**This paper should not be cited as an independent data source for any India-specific figures.** Its role in our manuscript is limited to: confirming that the sensitisation-reactivity paradox is recognised in the Indian literature; providing general allergen biology background; and noting that the paradox remains unexplained.

## Extracted points

> **Source quality note:** Full text read from PDF. All numbers below are from the paper but trace to primary sources that are also available in P04 and P02. Quotations are taken directly from the PDF.

### India-specific data (all citing the same underlying EuroPrevall-INCO studies)

1. "A food specific sensitization study conducted on children aged 7–10 years indicated that food allergy appears to be less common when compared with developed nations." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   Note: This refers to the EuroPrevall study, consistent with P02 and P04.

2. Food allergy prevalence in Indian children: 0.14%. — [FULL TEXT, citing reference 7 — DON'T KNOW which paper reference 7 is]

3. Food allergen sensitisation in Indian children: 19.1%. — [FULL TEXT, same reference]

4. Peanut allergy in Indian children: 0.03%; peanut sensitisation: 6.3%. — [FULL TEXT]

5. "Further examination is expected to explore why predominance of clinically applicable food sensitivity is low in India in spite of high sensitisation rate." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote — close paraphrase of the EuroPrevall paradox statement]

### Allergen biology content (not India-specific — general review)

6. Food allergens are proteins with molecular weight range "from 10 to 70 kDa." — [FULL TEXT]
   For us: Background characterisation relevant to the mechanism section.

7. Main protein families: cupins (seed storage proteins), prolamins (cereal storage proteins), tropomyosin (shellfish/crustacea muscle protein). — [FULL TEXT]
   For us: These families explain cross-reactivity patterns across legumes and across shellfish.

8. Global food allergy burden: affects over 200 million people worldwide. — [FULL TEXT, DON'T KNOW primary source year]

9. Western context: US childhood food allergy increased from 3.4% (1997–1999) to 5.1% (2009–2011). — [FULL TEXT]

10. "Current comprehension of the turn of the events and utilization of allergenic capability of certain types of food proteins is restricted." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
    "None of the current models has been approved, is prescient, or generally acknowledged with respect to their allergen explicit inhibitors." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]

### Allergens discussed in the paper

The paper covers the Big 8 allergen groups (peanuts, tree nuts, milk, egg, wheat, soy, fish, shellfish) as its primary frame. India-specific allergens beyond these are not discussed in detail. The paper does not address legumes specifically in the Indian context, eggplant, or any of the allergens identified in P05 (Bhattacharya 2018) as India-characterised.

## Gaps documented

- Lack of validated animal models for allergen-specific assessment
- Limited understanding of mechanisms driving sensitisation-reactivity paradox in India
- General limitation: most allergen characterisation data comes from Western populations

## Weight in manuscript

Low evidential weight for India-specific claims. The paper is useful only as a secondary confirming source for the consensus framing, and should not be cited in isolation for any numerical claim about Indian food allergy prevalence.
